pages.content:
### Visual extraction
- **Important visible text and headings**  
  - Blue header: "Job Aid"  
  - Main title: "Sample Checklists for Conducting Internal Monitoring and Auditing"  
  - Section heading: "Internal Monitoring" (blue)  
  - URL: `https://www.cms.gov/Medicare-Medicaid-Coordination/Fraud-Prevention/Medicaid-Integrity-Education/audit-toolkit.html`  
  - Logos: "U.S. DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH & HUMAN SERVICES" (seal) and "CMS CENTERS FOR MEDICARE & MEDICAID SERVICES"  

- **Tables/checklists/forms, with row or field meanings**  
  - Title references "Sample Checklists" but no checklist table is visible on this page.  

- **Diagrams/charts/logos/stamps/signatures and what they indicate**  
  - Calculator image (bottom left): Suggests numerical/auditing context.  
  - HHS and CMS logos (bottom right): Indicate official source (Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services).  

- **Key facts useful for search and question answering**  
  - Purpose: Guidance for internal monitoring/auditing of electronic health records (EHRs) to detect fraud, waste, abuse.  
  - References: HIPAA Privacy Rule (unauthorized EHR access), CMS requirements for managed care plans.  
  - Resource link: CMS Audit Toolkit for "Conducting a Self-Audit" guide.  
  - Page number "1" (bottom right).

### Visual extraction
- Important visible text and headings
  - "Figure 1. Relationship Between Monitoring and Auditing"
  - "Conclusion"
  - "Sample Checklists for Conducting Internal Monitoring and Auditing" (footer)
  - "Monitor and measure the efficacy of changes during the next regular audit cycle..."
  - "Providers can help detect and prevent fraud, waste, and abuse associated with EHRs..."

- Tables/checklists/forms, with row or field meanings
  - No traditional tables/checklists on this page; contains a process diagram with numbered steps

- Diagrams/charts/logos/stamps/signatures and what they indicate
  - "Figure 1. Relationship Between Monitoring and Auditing" diagram showing two converging pathways:
    - **MONITORING PROCESS** (left side, steps 01-05):
      - 01: IDENTIFY RISKS
      - 02: PERFORM ONE-TIME BASELINE AUDIT
      - 03: DEVELOP/UPDATE AN ONGOING MONITORING PLAN
      - 04: MONITOR RISKS (INCLUDING THOSE IDENTIFIED PREVIOUSLY BY MONITORING OR AUDITING)
      - 05: TAKE CORRECTIVE ACTION AND RECOGNIZE NEW RISKS
    - **AUDITING PROCESS** (right side, steps 07-10):
      - 07: IDENTIFY RISKS
      - 08: AUDIT RISK AREAS (INCLUDING THOSE IDENTIFIED BY PREVIOUS AUDITS AND MONITORING)
      - 09: REVIEW AUDIT RESULTS
      - 10: TAKE CORRECTIVE ACTION AND RECOGNIZE NEW RISKS
    - Center connection: 06 ADJUST MONITORING AND AUDITING PROCESSES
    - Labels showing "NEW RISKS IDENTIFIED THROUGH MONITORING" and "NEW RISKS IDENTIFIED THROUGH AUDITING"
    - Bottom text: "<<< NEW RISKS IDENTIFIED THROUGH AUDITING <<<"

- Key facts useful for search and question answering
  - Document focuses on EHR (Electronic Health Records) monitoring and auditing
  - Providers should establish processes to detect/prevent fraud, waste, and abuse in EHR systems
  - Monitoring and auditing processes form a continuous improvement cycle
  - CMS resource URL: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare-Medicaid-Coordination/Fraud-Prevention/Medicaid-Integrity-Education/edmic-landing.html
  - Part of "Electronic Health Records" Toolkit for Medicaid Program Integrity
  - Twitter: #MedicaidIntegrity

### Visual extraction
- "References" (blue heading at top)
- Footer: "Sample Checklists for Conducting Internal Monitoring and Auditing" (blue text) with page number "11"

- Numbered reference list (1-16) with consistent structure:
  - Entry number
  - Author/organization name
  - Publication date in parentheses
  - Document title
  - Source details (e.g., "Slide 26", "pp. 45-46")
  - Retrieval date (mostly "April 11, 2016")
  - Full URL (hyperlinked blue text)

- No diagrams, charts, logos, stamps, or signatures visible

- Key healthcare compliance references from:
  - Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) - multiple entries (1, 3, 14, 15, 16)
  - Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) of 1996 (entry 4)
  - 45 C.F.R. regulatory citations (entries 5, 6, 7, 8, 13)
  - Office of Inspector General (OIG) documents (entries 9, 12)
  - All references retrieved April 11-14, 2016
  - Multiple "Getting Started Webinar" references with different slide numbers (1, 3, 14)
  - URLs from cms.gov, ecfr.gov, gpo.gov, hhs.gov domains
  - Page part of "Sample Checklists for Conducting Internal Monitoring and Auditing" section

### Visual extraction
- **Page header**: Blue text at bottom: "Sample Checklists for Conducting Internal Monitoring and Auditing"
- **Page number**: "12" in blue at bottom right corner
- **Layout elements**: Yellow vertical stripe on left margin; blue vertical stripe on right margin
- **Content structure**: Numbered reference list (17-32) with consistent formatting pattern:
  - Entry number (left-aligned)
  - Authoring organization/author
  - Date in parentheses
  - Document title
  - Additional details (regulation numbers, page references)
  - Blue underlined hyperlinks
  - "Retrieved [date], from" pattern before each URL
- **Key visual patterns**: 
  - All references retrieved April 11-12, 2016
  - Repeated URLs (e.g., entry 17 and 22 share same OIG physician practices URL)
  - Entries 28 and 29 are identical references to McMillan et al. (2013)
  - Entries 31 and 32 reference identical CMS webinar but different slide numbers
- **Domain indicators**: 
  - Multiple U.S. Department of Health and Human Services references
  - CMS (Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services) documents
  - EHR/Health IT compliance references (ONC, HIPAA)
  - Incident reporting standards from multiple states (Wisconsin, California)
